_comment: Threads Hook 測試 — 純文字，10 天 x 每天 2 支。slot index 由 start_date + 當天時段自動計算。改 start_date 就能改測試起始日。
timezone_note: morning slot = 台灣 20:30 (UTC 12:30) / evening slot = 台灣 22:00 (UTC 14:00)

posts.text:
I spent $174 on AI tools last month.
It did the work of $2,400 in freelancers — voiceover, thumbnails, scripts, editing.
The math on "hire people" stopped making sense in 2025.

What's the one tool you'd pay $174/mo for without blinking?

I automated 100 social posts with Claude.
Here's what actually broke:
– captions all sounded the same after #30
– "best time to post" advice was useless
– volume ≠ growth; one good post beat 20 mid ones

Automation amplifies whatever you have. Weak angle = fail faster.
What broke first when you tried to automate?

Engineers are the most underpaid smart people in tech.
We build the thing that prints money, then watch sales and execs cash out.
AI just handed us the exit: build your OWN thing. No team, no permission.

Fellow engineers — what's stopping you from shipping your own product this year?

Your coding job probably has 5 good years left.
Not because AI replaces you — because it shrinks the team around you.
The engineers who win build a second income stream NOW, while they still have a salary to fund it.

Building while comfortable, or waiting until you have to?

My side project made $0 for 7 months.
Then I automated the boring 90% with AI, and month 8 made more than the first 7 combined.
Turns out I wasn't lazy. I was the bottleneck.

What's the one task secretly capping your income right now?

Day 1 of building in public:
My content pipeline works perfectly. Posts on schedule. Flawless images. Zero engagement.
Turns out "it runs" and "it works" are very different things.

This week I'm scrapping the automation and posting by hand to find out why.
Ever built something perfect that nobody wanted?

Designers got Figma. Writers got Substack.
Engineers got... a Jira ticket?
The same skills that make you "just a dev" make you dangerous solo with AI. We can build the whole funnel ourselves now.

What would you build if you needed zero other people?

The window where "AI side hustle" feels early is closing.
2 years ago: nobody believed it.
Today: skeptical but curious.
2 years out: saturated and obvious.

The best time to look slightly crazy was last year. Second best is today.
What's stopping you from starting this week?

A faceless YouTube video used to cost me 6 hours.
Now it's 22 minutes:
– Claude writes the script
– ElevenLabs does the voice
– one render command

6 hours → 22 min isn't "10% better." It's a different business.
How long does your content take, start to finish?

I spent 3 weeks building a content automation system before posting a single thing.
Classic engineer move: optimize the factory before checking if anyone wants the product.
Building the tool felt productive. It was procrastination with extra steps.

Guilty of this too? 🙋

Your manager calls it "job security."
I call it a single point of failure with a 2-week notice period.
I'm not quitting tomorrow. But I'm building so I never have to ask permission to be paid more.

Are you building a parachute, or hoping the plane lands?

Nobody is coming to give an engineer a raise that matches your real value.
You either ask (and get a number), or you build (and set your own).
I got tired of waiting for a "yes," so I started building a stream that needs no approval.

When's the last time your income went up without permission?

I make more from a $9 Notion template than from my first 3 months of freelancing.
Freelancing: trade hours for dollars, once.
Digital product: build once, sell while asleep.

I wish someone showed me this math at 25.
Are you still trading hours? Be honest.

My Threads engagement was flat for weeks.
The fix wasn't a clever algorithm hack.
It was replying to every comment in the first hour like an actual human.

The "growth hack" was just... talking to people.
What's the dumbest-simple thing that actually moved your needle?

Non-technical founders pay $5k for an MVP I can spin up in a weekend.
We undervalue the one skill everyone else outsources at a premium.
Stop building other people's dreams for a salary. Build yours on the side.

Engineers: what's your weekend-MVP idea? Drop it below.

AI didn't take your job.
But the engineer using AI to ship products on the side? They're quietly building leverage you don't have yet.
The gap isn't talent. It's who started.

Be honest — ahead of that curve, or behind it right now?

1,000 true fans × $99/mo = $99,000/mo.
Everyone quotes this number.
Nobody tells you fan #1 → #100 takes 2 years and breaks most people.

The math is easy. The patience isn't.
How many real fans do you actually have today? (I'll start: not many.)

Real numbers from my AI side project this month:
– hours worked: ~8
– tools cost: $174
– revenue: still figuring it out

I'm sharing the messy middle, not a success story. Most "passive income" threads skip this part because it's not sexy.
Where are you in the messy middle right now?

The "learn to code" crowd missed the memo.
The money was never in writing code.
It's in shipping a product people pay for — and AI removed every excuse you had about marketing, design, and copy.

What's the REAL reason you haven't shipped? (Mine was fear, not skill.)

In 5 years there'll be two kinds of engineers:
those who used AI to build income they own,
and those who used it to close Jira tickets 20% faster for the same salary.

Same tool. Only one changed their life.
Which one are you becoming?
